question: A woman with migraine now has headache on 20 days each month and alternates triptans with paracetamol-containing tablets. Before planning withdrawal and preventive therapy, the neurologist calculates whether each drug class has crossed the monthly exposure threshold for medication-overuse headache over more than three months. Which threshold pairing is correct?
options: Triptans at 10 days and simple analgesics at 10 days per month
Triptans at 10 days and simple analgesics at 15 days per month
Triptans at 15 days and simple analgesics at 15 days per month
Triptans at 15 days and simple analgesics at 10 days per month
correctAnswer: Triptans at 10 days and simple analgesics at 15 days per month
explanation: Medication overuse is defined at 10 or more days per month for triptans, opioids and combination analgesics, and at 15 or more days per month for simple analgesics, sustained for over three months in a patient with frequent headache. Mixed use can be overuse even when no single category appears dominant.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A woman with gastric-outlet obstruction has several days of vomiting, hypochloraemia, hypokalaemia and metabolic alkalosis. Even after the initial acid loss, volume contraction activates the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system and low distal chloride limits bicarbonate excretion, so the kidney perpetuates the disorder until saline and potassium are restored. Which mechanism best describes this maintenance phase?
options: Intestinal bicarbonate loss producing hyperchloraemic acidosis
Volume and chloride depletion sustaining renal bicarbonate retention
Failure of ammonium excretion producing uraemic acidosis
correctAnswer: Volume and chloride depletion sustaining renal bicarbonate retention
explanation: Vomiting generates alkalosis through gastric hydrogen loss, then chloride and volume depletion, hypokalaemia and secondary aldosterone activity maintain renal bicarbonate retention. Saline and potassium chloride correct these maintenance factors while the obstructive cause is treated.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old woman with diffuse systemic sclerosis develops abrupt headache, blood pressure of 208/118 mmHg, oliguria and a rapidly rising creatinine. The smear shows microangiopathic changes and there is no pulmonary oedema requiring a different first priority; renal replacement support is available if needed. Which antihypertensive class must be started promptly despite the rising creatinine?
explanation: Scleroderma renal crisis is treated immediately with an ACE inhibitor, classically captopril because it can be titrated rapidly, even if creatinine initially continues to rise. Other antihypertensives may be added for control, but they do not replace renin-angiotensin-system blockade; high-dose glucocorticoids can increase crisis risk.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 70-year-old man has severe calcific aortic stenosis, exertional chest pressure and no obstructive epicardial coronary disease on angiography. Echocardiography shows concentric left-ventricular hypertrophy with a high transvalvular gradient, and haemoglobin and oxygen saturation are normal. Which mechanism best explains his angina despite patent coronary arteries?
options: Hypertrophy-related demand with impaired subendocardial perfusion
Recurrent coronary embolisation from the stenotic valve
Increased diastolic pressure that augments coronary reserve
Reduced oxygen delivery caused solely by chronic anaemia
correctAnswer: Hypertrophy-related demand with impaired subendocardial perfusion
explanation: Pressure overload produces concentric hypertrophy, increased myocardial oxygen demand and elevated intramyocardial pressure that reduces subendocardial perfusion reserve. The resulting supply-demand mismatch can cause angina even when the epicardial coronary arteries are unobstructed.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 72-year-old woman with years of heavy combined-analgesic and NSAID use develops slowly progressive CKD, sterile pyuria and impaired concentrating ability. Imaging shows small irregular kidneys and papillary calcification rather than an abrupt haemodynamic creatinine rise, so the pathologist considers chronic analgesic nephropathy. Which renal lesion is characteristic?
options: Crescentic glomerulonephritis with linear immunoglobulin deposition
Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis with papillary necrosis
Minimal-change disease with diffuse podocyte effacement
correctAnswer: Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis with papillary necrosis
explanation: Chronic analgesic nephropathy is a tubulointerstitial process associated with renal papillary necrosis, reduced concentrating ability, sterile pyuria and small irregular kidneys. This differs from the acute afferent-vasoconstriction injury caused by NSAIDs during volume depletion.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 63-year-old man with levodopa-responsive Parkinson disease develops predictable bradykinesia and rigidity during the final hour before each levodopa dose. Adherence and gastric timing are optimized, and he has no troublesome dyskinesia, psychosis, or orthostatic hypotension. The neurologist wants to prolong the effect of each levodopa dose without using a dopamine-receptor blocker. Which add-on is most appropriate?
options: Haloperidol at the end of each dose interval
explanation: Entacapone inhibits peripheral catechol-O-methyltransferase and prolongs levodopa benefit, making it useful for predictable end-of-dose wearing off. Haloperidol is the closest centrally active drug option but blocks dopamine receptors and can markedly worsen parkinsonism.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 23-year-old athlete survives exertional heat stroke but has severe muscle pain, weakness and dark urine during observation. Because rhabdomyolysis can produce arrhythmia and pigment-associated kidney injury even after temperature normalises, fluid balance and ECG are followed while laboratory trends are repeated. Which group of tests is most important to monitor serially?
options: Thyrotropin, free thyroxine and morning cortisol
Amylase, lipase and faecal pancreatic elastase
Creatine kinase, potassium and renal function
correctAnswer: Creatine kinase, potassium and renal function
explanation: Creatine kinase tracks muscle injury, potassium identifies potentially lethal release from damaged myocytes and creatinine with urine output monitors acute kidney injury. Coagulation and liver tests are also important in severe heat stroke, but the stated muscle-pain and dark-urine syndrome specifically prioritises this rhabdomyolysis panel.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 37-year-old woman has fever, personality change, focal seizures and MRI evidence of temporal-lobe encephalitis. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis and red cells, and acyclovir has already been started because confirmation should not delay treatment. Which is the most appropriate first confirmatory assay on cerebrospinal fluid?
options: CSF anti-HSV immunoglobulin G antibody index
Paired serum and CSF total HSV antibody titres
explanation: CSF HSV polymerase chain reaction directly detects viral DNA and is the first confirmatory test of choice for HSV encephalitis. A very early negative result may require repeat PCR while acyclovir continues. Viral culture is insensitive and slow, while antibody-based CSF tests may support later or retrospective diagnosis but are not preferred for rapid initial confirmation.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 67-year-old man with newly diagnosed multiple myeloma has serum beta-two microglobulin of 6.1 mg/L and albumin of 3.7 g/dL. Lactate dehydrogenase is normal, and fluorescence in-situ hybridization shows no del(17p), t(4;14), or t(14;16). Kidney function is stable after hydration, and the team applies the Revised International Staging System rather than beta-two microglobulin alone. Which stage is assigned?
explanation: Although beta-two microglobulin at or above 5.5 mg/L makes ISS stage III, Revised ISS stage III additionally requires high-risk cytogenetics or elevated LDH. With neither adverse feature, the patient falls into Revised ISS stage II; stage I also fails because beta-two microglobulin is not below 3.5 mg/L.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: Several adults in a food-insecure community develop sensory-sparing spastic paraparesis after prolonged reliance on grass pea, supporting neurolathyrism. One patient, however, also has glossitis and impaired vibration sense, so a superimposed treatable nutritional myelopathy must not be missed. MRI excludes compression, and the team orders metabolic tests before attributing every deficit to irreversible toxin injury. Which additional result would most strongly support cobalamin-related subacute combined degeneration?
options: Normal methylmalonic acid with preserved vibration sense
Elevated methylmalonic acid with posterior-column sensory loss
correctAnswer: Elevated methylmalonic acid with posterior-column sensory loss
explanation: Elevated methylmalonic acid plus posterior-column dysfunction supports cobalamin deficiency and explains sensory findings that pure neurolathyrism should not cause. Preserved vibration sense is the closest expected toxin-related pattern and would not establish a superimposed treatable cobalamin myelopathy.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with thyroid storm is receiving beta-blockade, glucocorticoid and supportive care. The junior doctor proposes giving iodine before methimazole, but the endocrinologist insists that a thionamide must precede iodine by an appropriate interval because sequencing changes iodine physiology. What complication is this order intended to prevent?
options: Thionamide causing immediate release of stored thyroid hormone
Glucocorticoid accelerating peripheral conversion of T4 to T3
Iodine serving as substrate for new thyroid-hormone synthesis
correctAnswer: Iodine serving as substrate for new thyroid-hormone synthesis
explanation: A thionamide is given before iodine so organification and new hormone synthesis are blocked before an iodine load is supplied. Iodine given afterward acutely inhibits release of preformed hormone; the sequence is unrelated to glucocorticoid conversion effects or gastrointestinal absorption.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 34-year-old woman remains breathless despite a prescription for high-dose inhaled corticosteroid and long-acting bronchodilator. Before labelling the asthma severe, the respiratory clinic observes poor inhaler coordination, inconsistent refill history, workplace flour exposure and untreated rhinosinusitis, while spirometry is repeated to confirm variable obstruction. What should be completed before escalation to biologic therapy?
options: Confirm diagnosis, adherence, inhaler technique, exposures and comorbidities
Confirm eosinophil count, total IgE, sensitisation and exacerbation history
Confirm chest CT findings, sputum cultures, bronchoscopy and diffusion capacity
Confirm lung volumes, walking distance, oxygen need and pulmonary pressures
correctAnswer: Confirm diagnosis, adherence, inhaler technique, exposures and comorbidities
explanation: Before asthma is labelled severe, the diagnosis must be confirmed and modifiable causes of poor control must be addressed, especially adherence, inhaler technique, relevant exposures and comorbidities. Only persistent uncontrolled disease after that process is phenotyped with exacerbation history and biomarkers for an appropriate biologic. The alternative test bundles may be selectively useful when another diagnosis is suspected, but they do not replace the core difficult-to-treat asthma assessment.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with kidney failure has serum potassium of 6.9 mmol/L and receives intravenous calcium because the ECG shows QRS widening. Insulin with dextrose is then given while potassium removal by dialysis is arranged, and capillary glucose is monitored for delayed hypoglycaemia. What is the principal potassium-lowering action of insulin in this emergency?
options: It shifts extracellular potassium into cells
correctAnswer: It shifts extracellular potassium into cells
explanation: Insulin activates cellular sodium-potassium ATPase and rapidly shifts potassium into cells; dextrose limits treatment-induced hypoglycaemia. Calcium stabilises the cardiac membrane, while dialysis or functioning renal and gastrointestinal routes are required to remove potassium from the body.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A man with advanced AIDS presents with fever, wasting, mucosal ulcers, hepatosplenomegaly and pancytopenia, suggesting disseminated histoplasmosis but also tuberculosis. Cultures and tissue studies are obtained, yet the infectious-disease team needs a rapid, sensitive non-invasive test that performs particularly well when organism burden is high. Which test is most useful?
explanation: Histoplasma antigen detection in urine is rapid and sensitive in disseminated disease, especially in advanced HIV with a high fungal burden. Culture and histopathology remain important for confirmation, while tuberculin and galactomannan test different infections and one routine bacterial culture is not an adequate fungal test.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 56-year-old man with NSTE-ACS is scheduled for an invasive strategy and is prescribed ticagrelor with aspirin after bleeding risk and drug interactions are reviewed. He has not received another P2Y12 loading dose, and the team needs the standard acute loading regimen followed by maintenance during the first year. Which schedule is correct?
options: 300 mg loading dose, then 75 mg once daily
60 mg loading dose, then 10 mg once daily
90 mg loading dose, then 90 mg once daily
180 mg loading dose, then 90 mg twice daily
correctAnswer: 180 mg loading dose, then 90 mg twice daily
explanation: Ticagrelor is initiated with a 180-mg oral loading dose and then continued at 90 mg twice daily during the usual first 12 months after ACS when indicated. A 60-mg twice-daily dose may be used in selected extended therapy later, but the listed lower or once-daily schedules are not the standard acute regimen.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 74-year-old woman with new temporal headache and jaw claudication develops transient monocular visual loss followed by a pale swollen optic disc. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein are elevated, and temporal-artery ultrasound and biopsy are being arranged but cannot be completed immediately. Infection has been reasonably excluded, and the priority is preventing irreversible loss in the fellow eye. Which treatment should begin now?
options: Aspirin alone until biopsy confirms disease
Withhold therapy until permanent visual loss occurs
explanation: Suspected giant cell arteritis with visual ischemia requires immediate high-dose glucocorticoid therapy, often intravenous pulse treatment for threatened or established visual loss, without waiting for biopsy. Aspirin alone is the closest vascular treatment but cannot suppress the granulomatous arterial inflammation responsible for further ischemia.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 33-year-old woman has episodic headache, palpitations, sweating and paroxysmal hypertension, and ultrasound performed for abdominal pain incidentally shows an adrenal mass. Because imaging phenotype alone cannot establish catecholamine secretion and biopsy could be hazardous, biochemical confirmation is required before localisation and operative planning. Which initial screening test is preferred?
options: Plasma free or urinary fractionated metanephrines
correctAnswer: Plasma free or urinary fractionated metanephrines
explanation: Plasma free metanephrines or urinary fractionated metanephrines are sensitive initial biochemical tests for pheochromocytoma because catecholamines are continuously metabolised within the tumour. Cortisol suppression and urinary cortisol assess hypercortisolism, while the aldosterone-renin ratio screens for primary aldosteronism.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 34-year-old woman with migraine uses combination analgesics on 14 days each month and now has near-daily headache. Secondary causes have been excluded, and she fears that stopping the tablets will transiently worsen pain; education, rescue limits and follow-up are arranged. Which strategy best addresses the cause and reduces recurrence?
options: Taper the acute medicine and postpone prevention until withdrawal ends
Continue the current acute medicine while adding preventive therapy
Withdraw the overused medicine while optimising preventive therapy
Replace the combination analgesic with scheduled triptan therapy
correctAnswer: Withdraw the overused medicine while optimising preventive therapy
explanation: Medication-overuse headache requires education and withdrawal or marked restriction of the overused acute medicine. Preventive treatment can be started or optimised during withdrawal according to the clinical setting, with rescue limits and follow-up. Continuing the same exposure or replacing it with regularly scheduled triptan use perpetuates overuse; postponing prevention is not required and can leave frequent migraine untreated.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A postoperative patient develops sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic pain and tachycardia. Arterial blood gas shows acute respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation, but there is no wheeze, fever or obvious panic trigger, and the clinician refuses to label the symptoms as anxiety before assessing a potentially fatal vascular cause. Which diagnosis must be excluded urgently?
explanation: Pulmonary embolism commonly causes tachypnoea and acute respiratory alkalosis and must be considered in sudden postoperative dyspnoea with pleuritic pain and tachycardia. Anxiety is a diagnosis of exclusion in this context, while stable COPD more often tends toward carbon-dioxide retention.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 51-year-old woman has long-standing Raynaud phenomenon, sclerodactyly, telangiectasia and reflux symptoms, with skin thickening confined distal to the elbows. Spirometry is preserved but DLCO has begun to fall, so annual pulmonary-hypertension screening is intensified while the antibody profile is reviewed. Which autoantibody best matches this limited cutaneous phenotype and PAH risk?
explanation: Anticentromere antibodies are strongly associated with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis and a later risk of pulmonary arterial hypertension. Anti-topoisomerase I is more linked to interstitial lung disease, while anti-RNA polymerase III is associated with diffuse skin disease and scleroderma renal crisis.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 48-year-old woman with rheumatic mitral regurgitation has an apical holosystolic murmur radiating toward the axilla. Echocardiography confirms a regurgitant jet without dynamic outflow obstruction, and the examiner uses manoeuvres that alter afterload and venous return to reinforce the bedside diagnosis. Which manoeuvre should increase the murmur intensity?
options: Abrupt standing from a squatting position
Strain phase of the Valsalva manoeuvre
explanation: Sustained handgrip increases systemic vascular resistance and left-ventricular afterload, increasing regurgitant flow across an incompetent mitral valve. Valsalva strain and standing reduce preload, while inspiration predominantly augments right-sided murmurs.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with stage 3 CKD and heart failure develops gastroenteritis with poor intake while continuing an ACE inhibitor and diuretic, then takes ibuprofen for back pain. Creatinine rises abruptly with bland urine sediment, illustrating a combined loss of renal perfusion, efferent tone and prostaglandin-mediated afferent support. Which risk profile is most dangerous for NSAID-associated AKI?
options: Normal hydration in a healthy endurance athlete
Normal kidney function with one low ibuprofen dose
CKD with dehydration plus concurrent ACE inhibitor and diuretic therapy
Mild isolated hypertension with preserved renal function
correctAnswer: CKD with dehydration plus concurrent ACE inhibitor and diuretic therapy
explanation: CKD, volume depletion, diuretics and renin-angiotensin-system blockade create a high-risk haemodynamic setting in which NSAID afferent vasoconstriction can sharply reduce glomerular filtration. This combination is often called the triple-whammy interaction and warrants avoidance or close monitoring.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 68-year-old man with Parkinson disease has recurrent light-headedness on standing despite medication review, liberal fluid and salt intake, compression garments, and physical counter-manoeuvres. Supine blood pressure is 132/78 mmHg, standing pressure is 92/58 mmHg after three minutes, and there is no dehydration, bleeding, or arrhythmia. Symptoms now limit walking, while cognition and urinary emptying are adequate for monitored vasoconstrictor therapy. Which drug is an appropriate next step?
explanation: Midodrine is an alpha-1 agonist used for symptomatic neurogenic orthostatic hypotension when nonpharmacologic measures are insufficient, with monitoring for supine hypertension and urinary retention. Tamsulosin is the closest autonomic drug distractor but blocks alpha-1 receptors and can worsen orthostatic hypotension.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A marathon runner collapses with confusion, hypotension and a rectal temperature of 41.5 C on a humid day, yet his skin remains sweaty. Airway and circulation are supported, and renal, hepatic and coagulation monitoring begins, but the determinant of outcome is the speed at which core temperature falls. Which cooling method should be initiated immediately when feasible?
options: Passive rest in a warm, low-airflow room
explanation: Cold-water immersion provides the fastest effective cooling for exertional heat stroke and should begin immediately alongside resuscitation. Sweating does not exclude the diagnosis, antipyretics do not reset environmental hyperthermia, and dantrolene is not a substitute for rapid physical cooling.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 21-year-old with mild asthma has intermittent symptoms but remains at risk of severe exacerbation, and the clinician wants each rescue dose to deliver anti-inflammatory treatment rather than bronchodilator alone. Adherence and inhaler technique are reviewed, and the action plan is written around a reliever containing a rapid-onset bronchodilator plus inhaled corticosteroid. Which approach is preferred?
options: A short-acting beta-agonist used alone as reliever
An oral leukotriene antagonist used only during attacks
A long-acting beta-agonist used alone as reliever
explanation: An ICS-containing reliever, commonly low-dose ICS-formoterol where available and appropriate, treats bronchoconstriction while delivering corticosteroid at times of worsening inflammation and reduces severe exacerbations compared with SABA-only relief. LABA monotherapy is unsafe and an oral leukotriene drug is not a rapid reliever.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 64-year-old man with small-cell lung carcinoma has stable euvolaemic hyponatraemia of 128 mmol/L, low serum osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine and normal thyroid and adrenal function. He has no seizure, confusion or other severe neurological symptom, and oncology is treating the underlying tumour while sodium is monitored. What is the initial SIADH-directed measure?
options: Fluid restriction with monitored solute intake
correctAnswer: Fluid restriction with monitored solute intake
explanation: For chronic mild or moderate asymptomatic SIADH, fluid restriction is the usual initial measure while the cause and adequate dietary solute are addressed. Hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptoms, isotonic saline may worsen SIADH and desmopressin promotes rather than corrects water retention unless used to control overcorrection.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: An ART-naive adult begins treatment for smear-positive pulmonary tuberculosis without meningeal involvement. The HIV team uses the CD4 count to decide how urgently to begin antiretroviral therapy while checking rifamycin interactions, because the group with the greatest mortality benefit starts ART within about two weeks. Which CD4 result places the patient in that group?
options: CD4 count at or above 350 cells/uL
explanation: In pulmonary tuberculosis without meningitis, a CD4 count below 50 cells/uL supports starting ART within about two weeks after tuberculosis treatment begins. Higher counts generally allow initiation within the first several weeks, while tuberculous meningitis requires a different delay because CNS-IRIS can be harmful.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old man presents with intermittent left-sided chest discomfort and a normal initial ECG and troponin. He has no exertional pressure or autonomic symptoms, and the pain is sharply reproduced by palpating one costochondral junction, although serial assessment is still arranged because one finding cannot absolutely exclude ACS. Which feature most lowers the pre-test probability of an acute coronary syndrome?
options: A rise and fall in cardiac troponin
explanation: Precisely reproducible focal tenderness supports a musculoskeletal source and lowers the probability of ACS, though it does not by itself rule it out. Dynamic ischaemic ECG change, acute troponin kinetics and exertional pressure relieved by rest each increase concern for coronary ischaemia.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 32-year-old man who smokes heavily has rest pain, digital ulcers and migratory superficial thrombophlebitis. Proximal pulses are preserved, inflammatory markers and autoimmune testing are unremarkable, and angiography shows distal segmental occlusions with corkscrew collaterals rather than aortic-branch disease. Which vasculopathy is most likely?
explanation: Thromboangiitis obliterans is a tobacco-associated inflammatory thrombotic disease of distal small and medium vessels, often accompanied by superficial thrombophlebitis and preserved proximal pulses. Complete tobacco cessation is essential; Takayasu disease instead targets the aorta and major branches.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with pheochromocytoma is mistakenly given propranolol before alpha blockade and rapidly develops severe hypertension with pulmonary oedema. The endocrine and anaesthesia teams review catecholamine receptor physiology before stabilisation and surgery because the sequence, not simply the drug dose, caused the crisis. Which mechanism explains the deterioration?
options: Reflex activation of renal mineralocorticoid receptors
Selective blockade of adrenal cortisol synthesis
explanation: Beta blockade before adequate alpha blockade removes beta-2-mediated vasodilation while catecholamine-driven alpha vasoconstriction persists, causing a hypertensive crisis and potentially pulmonary oedema. Alpha blockade must therefore precede beta blockade when tachycardia still requires treatment.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 46-year-old woman with obesity and a previous myocardial infarction has six disabling migraine days each month despite appropriate acute non-vasoconstrictive therapy. She wants prevention, and the clinician considers cardiovascular safety, reproductive counselling, cognition and the effect of each drug on weight before choosing treatment. Which preventive is most likely to promote rather than oppose weight loss?
explanation: Topiramate is an evidence-based migraine preventive that commonly reduces appetite and weight, making it a reasonable option when its cognitive, renal-stone and teratogenic risks are addressed. Valproate, amitriptyline and flunarizine more often cause weight gain and have their own safety limitations.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 23-year-old woman hyperventilates after acute pain and develops perioral tingling and carpopedal spasm. Oxygen saturation and chest examination are normal, but an arterial blood gas is obtained to distinguish a primary respiratory process from metabolic alkalosis and mixed disease. Which result defines acute respiratory alkalosis?
options: High pH with low PaCO2 and a small bicarbonate fall
High pH with high bicarbonate and compensatory high PaCO2
Low pH with high PaCO2 and raised bicarbonate
Low pH with low bicarbonate and compensatory low PaCO2
correctAnswer: High pH with low PaCO2 and a small bicarbonate fall
explanation: Acute respiratory alkalosis has alkalaemia with a primary fall in PaCO2 and only a modest immediate fall in bicarbonate from buffering. Metabolic alkalosis primarily raises bicarbonate, respiratory acidosis raises PaCO2 with acidaemia and metabolic acidosis primarily lowers bicarbonate.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with Raynaud phenomenon, digital ulcers and sclerodactyly is being classified before organ-specific surveillance is planned. Skin thickening remains distal to the elbows, anticentromere antibody is present, lung volumes are preserved and DLCO is disproportionately reduced, raising concern for pulmonary vascular disease rather than extensive fibrosis. Which association is most accurate?
options: Renal crisis is most characteristic of longstanding limited disease
Limited anticentromere-positive disease is associated with PAH
Systemic-sclerosis antibodies do not correlate with organ patterns
Diffuse cutaneous disease is the main setting for isolated PAH
correctAnswer: Limited anticentromere-positive disease is associated with PAH
explanation: Limited cutaneous, anticentromere-positive systemic sclerosis carries an important risk of pulmonary arterial hypertension, often with a disproportionate fall in DLCO. Diffuse disease is more strongly associated with interstitial lung disease and anti-RNA-polymerase-III-related renal crisis, although individual phenotypes can overlap.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 52-year-old woman has severe primary mitral regurgitation with preserved left-ventricular ejection fraction and end-systolic diameter below the usual dysfunction thresholds. During follow-up she develops new atrial fibrillation and resting pulmonary-artery systolic pressure of 54 mmHg, while coronary disease and another cause of pulmonary hypertension are excluded. The valve is repairable and operative risk is low, although exertional symptoms remain modest. Which management is most appropriate?
options: Continue observation until ejection fraction is below 40%
Refer for mitral-valve repair despite preserved ventricular measurements
Use digoxin alone as definitive valve treatment
correctAnswer: Refer for mitral-valve repair despite preserved ventricular measurements
explanation: New atrial fibrillation or resting pulmonary hypertension in severe primary mitral regurgitation supports intervention when durable repair is likely, even before conventional LV dysfunction thresholds are reached. Observation is the closest alternative for an asymptomatic patient without adverse triggers, but these new complications signal harmful haemodynamic progression.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 25-year-old man with asthma has symptoms on most days, wakes twice weekly and finishes a reliever inhaler every month. Spirometry confirms variable airflow limitation, and the clinician checks adherence, technique, smoke exposure and rhinitis before changing controller therapy; there is no fixed obstruction to suggest COPD. How should his current asthma status be classified?
explanation: Frequent daytime symptoms, recurrent night waking and reliever overuse indicate uncontrolled asthma and increased exacerbation risk. Classification should trigger correction of modifiable factors and an ICS-containing treatment step-up rather than reliance on reliever use alone.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A malnourished patient with alcohol dependence has chronic sodium 106 mmol/L but only mild confusion. Potassium is low and liver disease is present, placing him at high risk for osmotic demyelination; sodium is checked frequently and desmopressin is available if a water diuresis causes overshoot. Which correction principle is safest?
options: Use slow correction within a strict daily upper limit
Normalise serum sodium completely within several hours
Give unrestricted hypertonic saline until symptoms disappear
Correct without accounting for potassium replacement
correctAnswer: Use slow correction within a strict daily upper limit
explanation: High-risk chronic hyponatraemia requires conservative correction with a strict daily limit and frequent monitoring because malnutrition, alcohol use, liver disease and hypokalaemia increase osmotic-demyelination risk. Potassium replacement itself can raise serum sodium and must be included in the correction calculation.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A patient with newly diagnosed HIV begins treatment for microbiologically confirmed tuberculosis. In most pulmonary and extrapulmonary forms ART is started early, but the team identifies one site where early immune reconstitution can increase intracranial inflammation, pressure and neurological injury, so initiation is deliberately delayed. Which form of tuberculosis creates this exception?
explanation: ART is delayed in tuberculous meningitis because CNS immune-reconstitution inflammation can worsen intracranial pathology and outcomes. Pulmonary tuberculosis and most non-CNS extrapulmonary disease are managed with earlier ART according to CD4 count and clinical guidance.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 64-year-old man with ischaemic cardiomyopathy remains in New York Heart Association class II after congestion has resolved. Left-ventricular ejection fraction is 30%, blood pressure is 112/70 mmHg, estimated glomerular filtration rate is 52 mL/min/1.73 m2, potassium is 4.6 mmol/L, and he has no diabetes. He already receives sacubitril-valsartan, carvedilol, and spironolactone at tolerated doses, while sinus rhythm is 66/min. Which additional medicine reduces heart-failure hospitalization and cardiovascular death irrespective of diabetes status?
explanation: An SGLT2 inhibitor such as dapagliflozin is one of the foundational disease-modifying therapies for HFrEF and improves outcomes whether or not diabetes is present. Ivabradine is the closest add-on distractor, but it requires sinus rhythm with a persistently elevated heart rate despite beta blockade, which this patient does not have.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 69-year-old man with severe smoking-related chronic obstructive pulmonary disease presents with increased dyspnoea and purulent sputum. After controlled oxygen, bronchodilators, systemic glucocorticoid, and antibiotics, arterial blood gas shows pH 7.26 and PaCO2 72 mmHg. He is alert, cooperative, haemodynamically stable, protecting his airway, and has neither copious secretions nor vomiting. Which ventilatory support should be instituted next?
options: Continuous positive airway pressure without pressure support
Immediate invasive ventilation without an NIV trial
explanation: Acute COPD exacerbation with persistent hypercapnic respiratory acidosis and no contraindication is an evidence-based indication for bilevel non-invasive ventilation, which reduces intubation and mortality. Immediate invasive ventilation is the closest escalation option but is reserved for NIV contraindication, failure, inability to protect the airway, or worsening instability.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 32-year-old woman has chronic loose stools, weight loss, iron-deficiency anaemia, and an intensely pruritic extensor-surface eruption. Total IgA is normal and IgA tissue-transglutaminase antibody is strongly positive while she is still eating gluten. Stool culture, thyroid testing, and pancreatic imaging are unrevealing, and no gluten-free diet has yet begun. Which investigation should establish the intestinal diagnosis in this adult?
options: Upper endoscopy with multiple duodenal biopsies
Faecal elastase followed by pancreatic MRI
correctAnswer: Upper endoscopy with multiple duodenal biopsies
explanation: In an adult with positive coeliac serology, upper endoscopy with multiple biopsies from the duodenal bulb and distal duodenum confirms characteristic enteropathy before treatment alters the mucosa. Colonoscopic biopsy is the closest tissue-based alternative but does not sample the proximal small bowel where coeliac injury is assessed.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 45-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer develops rapidly progressive dyspnoea, confusion, and hypotension. Jugular venous pressure is elevated, heart sounds are quiet, pulsus paradoxus is 18 mmHg, and echocardiography shows a large effusion with right-sided diastolic chamber collapse. A cautious crystalloid bolus is being used only as a bridge while the procedural team arrives. Which intervention is now definitive and urgent?
explanation: Haemodynamically unstable cardiac tamponade requires urgent pericardial drainage, usually echo-guided pericardiocentesis with a catheter when feasible. Loop diuresis is the closest treatment for a congested patient, but reducing preload in tamponade can worsen output because ventricular filling is already externally constrained.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 58-year-old man with community-acquired pneumonia remains febrile despite appropriate antibiotics and has a moderate freely accessible pleural effusion. Ultrasound-guided aspiration yields cloudy non-purulent fluid with immediate pH 7.08, low glucose, and high lactate dehydrogenase; Gram stain is negative. He is not anticoagulated and the collection can be entered safely under imaging guidance. Which pleural intervention is indicated now?
options: Repeat diagnostic aspiration after one week
explanation: A parapneumonic effusion with pH at or below 7.2 is high risk for complicated pleural infection and should be drained with an image-guided intercostal tube when safely accessible. Repeat aspiration and observation are the closest conservative alternatives but allow loculation and sepsis to progress despite a drainage-level biochemical result.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.

subject: Medicine
question: A 61-year-old smoker has a central lung mass, mediastinal adenopathy, and syndrome of inappropriate antidiuresis; biopsy confirms limited-stage small-cell lung cancer. He has good performance status and completes concurrent platinum-etoposide chemotherapy with thoracic radiotherapy without progression. Brain MRI shows no metastasis, and autoimmune disease does not preclude checkpoint inhibition. Which post-chemoradiation systemic strategy reflects the current treatment advance?
explanation: Durvalumab consolidation improves progression-free and overall survival after non-progressing concurrent platinum-based chemoradiation for limited-stage small-cell lung cancer and is now an approved standard. Observation was the historical closest option, while osimertinib targets EGFR-mutated non-small-cell lung cancer rather than this histology.
source: StudyDoc 2026 PYT-theme question bank; references and image provenance are included with this item.
